topics.title: Explore word embeddings using Word2Vec, GloVe, and Doc2Vec, and visualize word embeddings to analyse their effectiveness in capturing semantic relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCML607 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Explore word embeddings using Word2Vec, GloVe, and Doc2Vec, and visualize word embeddings to analyse their effectiveness in capturing semantic relationships # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Word Embeddings? **Word Embeddings** are dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector representations of words that capture their semantic, syntactic, and contextual meaning in a continuous vector space. Unlike traditional one-hot encoding (sparse, high-dimensional), embeddings map semantically similar words to nearby points in vector space. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** *Word embeddings are distributed representations of words where each word is mapped to a dense vector of real numbers such that words with similar meanings have similar vector representations.* **Mathematical Form:** For a vocabulary $V$ of size $\vert V \vert$, each word $w \in V$ is mapped to a vector $\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d$ is the embedding dimension (typically 50, 100, 200, or 300). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Map of Meaning" Imagine you have a **giant map of a city**. On this map: - **Hospitals** cluster in one district. - **Schools** cluster in another. - **Restaurants** form a third cluster. - And surprisingly, **"King" and "Queen"** sit in the "Royalty" district, while **"Man" and "Woman"** sit in a different but parallel district. Word embeddings work exactly like this city map. Each word becomes a point (a "house") on the map. Words used in similar contexts live close to each other. The famous equation: $$\vec{King} - \vec{Man} + \vec{Woman} \approx \vec{Queen}$$ means: *"If you take the Royalty district and shift it from Male-direction to Female-direction, you land on Queen."* This is the magic of embeddings — they encode **meaning as geometry**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why not One-Hot?** > In one-hot encoding, "cat" = [1,0,0,...] and "dog" = [0,1,0,...]. The dot product is **0**, so the model thinks they are equally unrelated to "car". Embeddings solve this by giving "cat" and "dog" a non-zero cosine similarity because they share semantic context (both are pets, both are animals, both have four legs). --- ## 1.3 The Three Key Embedding Techniques | Technique | Year | Type | Key Idea | |---|---|---|---| | **Word2Vec** | 2013 (Mikolov, Google) | Predictive | Train a shallow neural network to predict context (or word from context) | | **GloVe** | 2014 (Pennington, Stanford) | Count-based | Factorize the global word-word co-occurrence matrix | | **Doc2Vec** | 2014 (Mikolov, Google) | Predictive Extension | Adds a **paragraph/document vector** so entire documents get embeddings | > [!NOTE] > **Dimension Hyperparameters (commonly used):** > - Vector size **d = 100** (default in `gensim`) > - Context window **w = 5** (default) > - Minimum word count **min_count = 5** > - Training algorithm: **CBOW** (fast) or **Skip-gram** (better for rare words) # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Word2Vec — The Predictive Model Word2Vec is a **shallow two-layer neural network** trained for one task: predicting nearby words. It comes in two flavors: ### 2.1.1 Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW) **Concept:** Predict the **center (target) word** from a window of surrounding **context words**. **Architecture flow:** 1. Input: One-hot vectors of $2c$ context words (where $c$ = window size). 2. Embedding layer: Multiply by shared weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$, average the rows. 3. Output: Softmax over vocabulary to predict the center word. **Objective (to maximize):** $$\mathcal{L}_{CBOW} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{t-c} : w_{t+c})$$ ### 2.1.2 Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS) **Concept:** Predict each **context word** given a single **center word**. To make it efficient, instead of updating all $\vert V \vert$ weights per step, we sample a few **negative examples** (random words that are NOT the true context). **Objective (to maximize):** $$\mathcal{L}_{SGNS} = \sum_{(w,c) \in D} \log \sigma(\vec{v}_w \cdot \vec{u}_c) + \sum_{(w,c') \in D'} \log \sigma(-\vec{v}_w \cdot \vec{u}_{c'})$$ Where: - $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ is the sigmoid function. - $D$ = set of observed (word, context) pairs (positive samples). - $D'$ = set of randomly sampled negative pairs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why SGNS works so well:** It reformulates word embedding learning as a **binary classification** — "Is this word-context pair real, or fake?" This makes it computationally cheap and produces high-quality embeddings. --- ## 2.2 GloVe — Global Vectors for Representation GloVe stands on a simple intuition: **the ratio of co-occurrence probabilities encodes meaning**. ### 2.2.1 The Core Insight Consider three words: $i$ = "ice", $j$ = "steam", $k$ = "solid". - $P(k \mid i)$ = probability "solid" appears near "ice" → **high** - $P(k \mid j)$ = probability "solid" appears near "steam" → **low** - The **ratio** $P(k \mid i) / P(k \mid j)$ is **large**, so "solid" is more related to "ice". This ratio is a much better discriminator than raw probabilities! ### 2.2.2 The GloVe Objective $$\mathcal{J} = \sum_{i,j=1}^{\vert V \vert} f(X_{ij}) \left( \vec{w}_i^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij} \right)^2$$ Where: - $X_{ij}$ = number of times word $j$ occurs in context of word $i$. - $\vec{w}_i$ = word vector for target $i$. - $\tilde{\vec{w}}_j$ = separate context word vector for $j$. - $b_i, \tilde{b}_j$ = scalar biases. - $f(X_{ij})$ = weighting function (caps influence of very common words like "the", "a"). The weighting function is defined as: $$f(x) = \begin{cases} (x/x_{max})^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ Typical values: $x_{max} = 100$, $\alpha = 0.75$. > [!NOTE] > **Word2Vec vs GloVe:** Word2Vec is **local** (uses a sliding window, one pair at a time). GloVe is **global** (uses the entire co-occurrence matrix statistics at once). GloVe often performs better on word analogy tasks because it captures global statistics. --- ## 2.3 Doc2Vec — Embedding Whole Documents Standard Word2Vec only embeds **words**. Doc2Vec (also called Paragraph Vectors) extends this to **variable-length pieces of text** (sentences, paragraphs, documents). ### 2.3.1 Two Variants | Variant | Full Name | Concept | |---|---|---| | **PV-DM** | Paragraph Vector — Distributed Memory | Concatenate (or average) paragraph vector + word vectors → predict next word | | **PV-DBOW** | Paragraph Vector — Distributed Bag of Words | Paragraph vector alone → predict random words sampled from the paragraph | **PV-DM Objective:** $$\mathcal{L}_{PV-DM} = \sum_{d \in D} \sum_{t} \log P(w_t \mid w_{t-c:t+c}, \vec{d})$$ Where $\vec{d}$ is the unique document/paragraph vector for document $d$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Doc2Vec is widely used in **document classification, sentiment analysis, and recommendation systems** because each document gets a fixed-size vector regardless of its length. --- ## 2.4 Visualization Techniques (t-SNE & PCA) High-dimensional vectors ($d=100$) cannot be plotted directly. We need **dimensionality reduction**: ### 2.4.1 PCA (Principal Component Analysis) Linear projection onto the top-2 principal components. $$Y = X W_{2}$$ where $W_{2} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 2}$ contains the top-2 eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. ### 2.4.2 t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) Non-linear method that preserves **local neighborhoods**. **Step 1 — Compute pairwise similarities in high-D using Gaussian distribution:** $$p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp\left(-\frac{\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\left(-\frac{\lVert x_i - x_k \rVert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)}$$ Symmetrize: $p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}$ **Step 2 — In low-D space, use Student t-distribution (heavy tails):** $$q_{ij} = \frac{\left(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^2\right)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}\left(1 + \lVert y_k - y_l \rVert^2\right)^{-1}}$$ **Step 3 — Minimize Kullback-Leibler divergence via gradient descent:** $$\mathcal{L}_{tSNE} = KL(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$$ --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $V$ | Vocabulary size | 10,000 – 1,000,000 | | $d$ | Embedding dimension | 50 / 100 / 200 / 300 | | $c$ | Context window size | 2 – 10 (default = 5) | | $X_{ij}$ | Co-occurrence count (GloVe) | Integer | | $\sigma(\cdot)$ | Sigmoid activation | $(0, 1)$ range | | $\alpha$ | GloVe weighting exponent | **0.75** | | $x_{max}$ | GloVe weight cutoff | **100** | | $f(X_{ij})$ | GloVe weighting function | Caps at 1 | | $b_i, \tilde{b}_j$ | Bias terms (GloVe) | Scalars | | $N$ | Negative samples (Word2Vec) | 5 – 20 | | $\eta$ | Learning rate | 0.025 → 0 (decaying) | | $epochs$ | Training passes | 5 – 50 | | Cosine similarity $\cos(\vec{a}, \vec{b})$ | Similarity metric $[-1, 1]$ | $\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\Vert \vec{a} \Vert \Vert \vec{b} \Vert}$ | | Perplexity | t-SNE hyperparameter | **5 – 50** | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Semantic search beyond keyword matching. - **Recommendation Systems (Netflix, Spotify):** Embedding items/users into shared space. - **Sentiment Analysis:** Embeddings capture positive/negative polarity. - **Chatbots & LLMs (BERT, GPT):** Modern transformers learn contextual embeddings. - **Healthcare:** Embedding medical terms to find related drugs/symptoms. - **Fraud Detection:** Embedding transaction sequences to detect anomalies. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: From One-Hot to Dense Embedding (Linear Projection) **Problem:** Show how a one-hot vector $\vec{x}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ is transformed into a dense embedding $\vec{v}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ via the embedding matrix $W$. **Step 1 — One-hot representation of word $w$:** $$\vec{x}_w = [0, 0, \ldots, 0, \underbrace{1}_{\text{position } i}, 0, \ldots, 0]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$$ **Step 2 — Embedding matrix definition:** $$W = \begin{bmatrix} - & \vec{r}_1 & - \\ - & \vec{r}_2 & - \\ & \vdots & \\ - & \vec{r}_{\vert V \vert} & - \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$$ where $\vec{r}_i$ is the embedding row for the $i$-th word. **Step 3 — Multiplication:** $$\vec{v}_w = W^T \vec{x}_w$$ Since $\vec{x}_w$ has a 1 only at position $i$, this picks out the $i$-th row of $W$: $$\vec{v}_w = \vec{r}_i$$ **Conclusion:** The embedding matrix multiplication is essentially a **row lookup**. The $i$-th row of $W$ is the embedding of the $i$-th vocabulary word. This is exactly what `nn.Embedding` does in PyTorch and what `model.wv[word]` returns in Gensim. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Skip-gram Negative Sampling Loss (Full Derivation) **Setup:** We have a center word $w$ and a true context word $c$. The model assigns them vectors $\vec{v}_w$ and $\vec{u}_c$. We want $P(D=1 \mid w, c) = \sigma(\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_c)$ to be high for real pairs and low for negative samples. **Step 1 — Probability of a real (positive) pair:** $$P(D=1 \mid w, c) = \sigma(\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_c) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_c}}$$ **Step 2 — Probability of a fake (negative) pair:** $$P(D=0 \mid w, c') = 1 - \sigma(\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_{c'}) = \sigma(-\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_{c'})$$ **Step 3 — Likelihood over one positive and $k$ negative samples:** $$\mathcal{L} = \log \sigma(\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_c) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{c' \sim P_n(w)} \left[ \log \sigma(-\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_{c'}) \right]$$ **Step 4 — Loss to minimize (negative of log-likelihood):** $$\mathcal{J} = -\log \sigma(\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_c) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log \sigma(-\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_{c'})$$ **Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $\vec{v}_w$ (for one positive sample):** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \vec{v}_w} = \left[ \sigma(\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_c) - 1 \right] \vec{u}_c + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma(\vec{v}_w^T \vec{u}_{c'_i}) \vec{u}_{c'_i}$$ This gradient pushes the center word's vector **toward** true context vectors and **away** from negative samples — exactly the desired geometric effect. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: GloVe's Meaning-from-Ratio Argument **Setup:** Define $P_{ik} = \frac{X_{ik}}{X_i}$ = probability that word $k$ appears in context of word $i$. **Step 1 — Consider ratio $R = P_{ik} / P_{jk}$ for probe word $k$ with target words $i, j$:** **Case 1:** $k$ is related to $i$ but not $j$ (e.g., $i$=ice, $j$=steam, $k$=solid): $$R = \frac{\text{large}}{\text{small}} = \text{large} \gg 1$$ **Case 2:** $k$ is related to $j$ but not $i$ (e.g., $k$=gas): $$R = \frac{\text{small}}{\text{large}} = \text{small} \ll 1$$ **Case 3:** $k$ is unrelated to both (e.g., $k$=the, $k$=fashion): $$R = \frac{\text{small}}{\text{small}} \approx 1$$ **Step 2 — We want a function $F$ that captures this ratio using word vectors:** $$F(\vec{w}_i, \vec{w}_j, \tilde{\vec{w}}_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}$$ **Step 3 — On the left side, word vectors can interact only via dot product (vectors live in $\mathbb{R}^d$):** $$F((\vec{w}_i - \vec{w}_j)^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}$$ **Step 4 — Homomorphism property:** If $F(x) = \exp(x)$, then: $$F((\vec{w}_i - \vec{w}_j)^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k) = \exp(\vec{w}_i^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k - \vec{w}_j^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k) = \frac{\exp(\vec{w}_i^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k)}{\exp(\vec{w}_j^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k)}$$ **Step 5 — Matching both sides:** $$\exp(\vec{w}_i^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k) = P_{ik} = \frac{X_{ik}}{X_i}$$ $$\vec{w}_i^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k = \log(X_{ik}) - \log(X_i)$$ **Step 6 — Introduce biases $b_i$ to absorb $\log(X_i)$ and get the final objective:** $$\vec{w}_i^T \tilde{\vec{w}}_k + b_i + \tilde{b}_k = \log(X_{ik})$$ Taking squared error with weighting $f(X_{ik})$ gives the GloVe loss $\mathcal{J}$ from Section 2.2.2. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Embedding Comparison | Property | Word2Vec (CBOW) | Word2Vec (Skip-gram) | GloVe | Doc2Vec | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Type** | Predictive | Predictive | Count-based | Predictive | | **Training Data Use** | Local window | Local window | Global co-occurrence | Per-paragraph | | **Speed** | Fast (faster than Skip-gram) | Moderate | Moderate | Slowest | | **Quality on rare words** | Poor | Good | Good | Good | | **Quality on frequent words** | Good | Good | Excellent | Good | | **Output** | Word vectors | Word vectors | Word vectors | Word + Doc vectors | | **Best Use Case** | Large corpora, real-time | Large corpora, analogies | Smaller corpora, global stats | Document-level tasks | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Cosine Similarity **Problem:** Given two 4-D embedding vectors, compute their cosine similarity. $$\vec{a} = [1, 2, 3, 4], \quad \vec{b} = [4, 3, 2, 1]$$ **Step 1 — Dot product:** $$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = (1)(4) + (2)(3) + (3)(2) + (4)(1) = 4 + 6 + 6 + 4 = 20$$ **Step 2 — Norms:** $$\Vert \vec{a} \Vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{30} \approx 5.477$$ $$\Vert \vec{b} \Vert = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{30} \approx 5.477$$ **Step 3 — Cosine similarity:** $$\cos(\theta) = \frac{20}{\sqrt{30} \cdot \sqrt{30}} = \frac{20}{30} = 0.667$$ **Interpretation:** A cosine of **0.667** indicates the vectors point in roughly the same direction — they are **semantically related**. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Word2Vec CBOW Architecture (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Context Word 1
