- 🔍 **Grid Search + K-Fold CV:** Standard KTU-recommended method. $K = 5$ is the default; $K = 10$ for smaller datasets.
- 🧮 **Sigmoid Function:** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ — the cornerstone of Logistic Regression.
- 📉 **Soft Margin SVM:** $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ — the objective being tuned.
- 🔬 **RBF Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ — sensitive to $\gamma$ tuning.
- 📏 **Metrics to Report:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, ROC-AUC — KTU expects *multiple* metrics, not just accuracy.
- 🚫 **Zero-Frequency Problem:** The fundamental reason MNB needs smoothing ($\alpha > 0$).
- 🧠 **Model Selection Rule:** $\hat{\mathbf{h}}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{h}} \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} M_k(\mathbf{h})$.
- 💡 **Standard Deviation:** Always report alongside mean CV score to assess model stability.
- 🎯 **Sentiment-Specific Insight:** Rare negation cues ("awful", "boring") benefit from small $\alpha$; class imbalance benefits from F1 over accuracy.
- 📚 **Computational Tip:** Linear SVM scales best for large sentiment corpora; MNB is fastest for small datasets.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Hyper-parameter Tuning for Sentiment Classification**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Develop a Named Entity Recognition (NER) system using sequence labelling techniques that handles ambiguity in NER, and evaluate the NER model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCML607 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Develop a Named Entity Recognition (NER) system using sequence labelling techniques that handles ambiguity in NER, and evaluate the NER model # Module Experiment 2: Named Entity Recognition (NER) Using Sequence Labelling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** is a sequence labelling task in Natural Language Processing (NLP) that seeks to locate and classify named entities mentioned in unstructured text into predefined categories such as **PERSON (PER)**, **LOCATION (LOC)**, **ORGANIZATION (ORG)**, **DATE**, **TIME**, **MONEY**, **PERCENT**, and **MISCELLANEOUS (MISC)**. It is formally defined as the problem of mapping an input token sequence $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ to a corresponding label sequence $Y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)$ where each $y_i \in \mathcal{L}$, the set of valid entity tags. NER is a foundational subtask of **Information Extraction (IE)** and is the first step in building downstream NLP pipelines such as **Question Answering**, **Relation Extraction**, **Coreference Resolution**, and **Knowledge Graph Construction**. ### 1.2 Sequence Labelling — The Core Paradigm Sequence labelling models output **one label per token**, preserving the sequential nature of the text. Unlike classification, the prediction at position $i$ depends on both the local features of $x_i$ and the labels of neighbouring tokens (a key property that makes NER a structured prediction problem, not mere independent token classification). > [!NOTE] > **Why is NER a Sequence Labelling Problem and not a plain classification problem?** > Because the label of a token depends on the labels around it. For example, the word "Washington" could be a *Person* (George Washington), a *Location* (Washington D.C.), or an *Organization* (Washington Post). Only by observing neighbouring context (and the labels of nearby tokens) can a model resolve this ambiguity. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Newspaper Highlighter" > [!TIP] > **Intuition:** Imagine you are a librarian handed a stack of raw newspaper articles. Your job is to physically highlight every mention of a person in **yellow**, every place in **green**, and every company in **blue**. Now, a particularly tricky article mentions "Apple announced its new iPhone." — is *Apple* a fruit or the company? You glance at the surrounding words ("announced", "iPhone") and the previous sentence (a stock-market report) — and you confidently highlight it blue. This contextual reasoning is *exactly* what an NER model performs: it reads the sentence holistically and uses sequential context to disambiguate meaning. ### 1.4 Tagging Schemes Used in NER NER systems encode multi-token entity boundaries using a tagging scheme. The three dominant schemes are: | Scheme | Tag Pattern | Example: "Barack Obama visited Paris" | |--------|-------------|----------------------------------------| | **BIO** | `B-XXX, I-XXX, O` | `B-PER, I-PER, O, B-LOC` | | **BIOES** | `B-XXX, I-XXX, E-XXX, S-XXX, O` | `B-PER, E-PER, O, S-LOC` | | **IOB1** | `I-XXX, O` (no B-prefix) | `I-PER, I-PER, O, I-LOC` | Where: - **B-XXX** = Beginning of an entity of type XXX - **I-XXX** = Inside (continuation) of an entity - **E-XXX** = End of an entity (only in BIOES) - **S-XXX** = Singleton (single-token entity, only in BIOES) - **O** = Outside any entity > [!IMPORTANT] > The **BIO scheme** is the most widely used in practice and is the default for the **CoNLL-2003** shared task, which is the standard benchmark for evaluating NER systems. ### 1.5 Sources of Ambiguity in NER NER ambiguity arises when a token's entity class is unclear from local context alone. The major ambiguity types are: 1. **Lexical Ambiguity** — "Apple" (fruit vs. company), "Amazon" (river vs. e-commerce firm). 2. **Syntactic Ambiguity** — "French books" (nationality-modifier vs. language-modifier). 3. **Discourse / Long-range Ambiguity** — Pronouns and references spanning multiple sentences. 4. **Boundary Ambiguity** — Is "New York University" a single ORG or ORG + LOC? 5. **Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) Ambiguity** — Unseen words during inference that resemble known entity classes. 6. **Nested Entity Ambiguity** — "University of Kerala" contains "Kerala" (LOC) inside an ORG. These ambiguities are precisely what **sequence labelling** with **contextual models** (BiLSTM, BERT) is designed to resolve. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of NER Sequence Labelling Models NER sequence labelling has evolved through three architectural generations. KTU 2024 expects you to know all three and justify your experimental choice. ### Pillar 1 — Hidden Markov Model (HMM) — Generative Baseline An HMM models the joint probability $P(X, Y)$ of observation sequence $X$ and label sequence $Y$. It is governed by: - **Transition probability:** $P(y_t \mid y_{t-1})$ — how likely one tag follows another. - **Emission probability:** $P(x_t \mid y_t)$ — how likely a word appears given a tag. - **Initial probability:** $\pi_i = P(y_1 = i)$. The decoding problem (finding the best tag sequence) is solved by the **Viterbi algorithm**, a dynamic-programming approach that finds: $$Y^* = \arg\max_{Y} P(Y \mid X) = \arg\max_{Y} P(X, Y)$$ **Limitation:** HMM assumes *strong independence* (each observation depends only on current state), so it cannot use rich features like capitalization, suffixes, or word embeddings. This is why it struggles with ambiguous entities. ### Pillar 2 — Conditional Random Field (CRF) — Discriminative Workhorse CRF directly models the conditional probability $P(Y \mid X)$ and can incorporate arbitrary, overlapping local features. For a linear-chain CRF (the variant used in NER): $$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{1}{Z(X)} \exp\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y_{t-1}, y_t, X, t) \right)$$ Where: - $f_k(y_{t-1}, y_t, X, t)$ are **feature functions** (transition features + emission features). - $\lambda_k$ are learned weights. - $Z(X)$ is the **partition function** that normalizes the score over all possible label sequences: $$Z(X) = \sum_{Y'} \exp\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y'_{t-1}, y'_t, X, t) \right)$$ **Why CRF excels at NER ambiguity:** It uses *global normalization* — the score of a label sequence is computed in its entirety, which prevents the model from producing invalid sequences like `I-PER` following `O`. This is a powerful constraint that handles **boundary ambiguity**. ### Pillar 3 — BiLSTM-CRF (and BERT) — Neural SOTA The neural era replaced handcrafted features with **learned dense representations**: 1. **Embedding layer** — word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) or contextual embeddings (BERT). 2. **BiLSTM layer** — captures past and future context for each token: $$\overrightarrow{h_t} = \text{LSTM}(x_t, \overrightarrow{h_{t-1}}), \quad \overleftarrow{h_t} = \text{LSTM}(x_t, \overleftarrow{h_{t+1}})$$ $$h_t = [\overrightarrow{h_t} \, ; \, \overleftarrow{h_t}]$$ 3. **CRF layer** — sits on top of BiLSTM outputs to enforce valid label transitions. For **BERT-based NER**, the BiLSTM is replaced by a **Transformer encoder** that produces contextual embeddings; the final token representations are projected to tag-space and fed into a CRF head. ## 2.2 Handling Ambiguity — Mechanism-by-Mechanism | Ambiguity Type | How Sequence Labelling Resolves It | |----------------|-------------------------------------| | **Lexical (Apple = fruit vs. company)** | BiLSTM/BERT use bidirectional context to read surrounding words ("iPhone", "CEO Tim Cook"). | | **Boundary (New York University)** | CRF transition constraints forbid invalid B-/I- tag combinations. | | **OOV (unseen proper noun)** | Character-level embeddings / subword tokenization (BERT WordPiece) handle unseen words. | | **Long-range (pronouns)** | Self-attention (in Transformer) connects distant tokens. | | **Nested entities** | Span-based models or layered tagging (advanced; beyond KTU scope). | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table contains the mathematically verifiable equations required for the KTU 2024 ESE on NER sequence labelling. Memorize all of them.** | # | Concept | Formula | Use Case | |---|---------|---------|----------| | 1 | HMM joint probability | $P(X,Y) = \pi_{y_1} \prod_{t=2}^{n} P(y_t \mid y_{t-1}) \prod_{t=1}^{n} P(x_t \mid y_t)$ | Generative NER baseline | | 2 | Viterbi recursion | $\delta_t(j) = \max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(x_t) \right]$ | HMM decoding | | 3 | Viterbi back-pointer | $\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right]$ | Tag sequence recovery | | 4 | Linear-chain CRF | $P(Y \mid X) = \dfrac{\exp\!\left( \sum_{t,k} \lambda_k f_k \right)}{Z(X)}$ | Discriminative NER | | 5 | CRF partition function | $Z(X) = \sum_{Y'} \exp\!\left( \sum_{t,k} \lambda_k f_k \right)$ | Normalization | | 6 | CRF negative log-likelihood loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i} \log P(Y^{(i)} \mid X^{(i)})$ | Training objective | | 7 | BiLSTM forward pass | $\overrightarrow{h_t} = \text{LSTM}(x_t, \overrightarrow{h_{t-1}})$ | Past context | | 8 | BiLSTM backward pass | $\overleftarrow{h_t} = \text{LSTM}(x_t, \overleftarrow{h_{t+1}})$ | Future context | | 9 | BiLSTM concatenation | $h_t = [\overrightarrow{h_t} ; \overleftarrow{h_t}]$ | Final token rep. | | 10 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Evaluation | | 11 | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Evaluation | | 12 | F1-score (micro) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | Evaluation | | 13 | Token Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{\text{correct tags}}{\text{total tags}}$ | Quick check | | 14 | BERT input embedding | $E_t = TE_t + SE_t + PE_t$ | Token + Segment + Position | > [!WARNING] > The KTU examiner will look for the **Viterbi recursion** (Formula 2) and the **CRF loss** (Formula 6) in any 14-mark question on this experiment. Forgetting the **partition function** $Z(X)$ in Formula 4 is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. ## 2.4 Evaluation Metrics for the NER Model NER evaluation is **entity-level**, not token-level. The standard practice (CoNLL-2003) is: - **True Positive (TP):** an entity span that is predicted *exactly* (both boundary and type) as in the gold standard. - **False Positive (FP):** a predicted entity that does not exist in gold. - **False Negative (FN):** a gold entity that the model missed. > [!IMPORTANT] > In **strict evaluation** (used by `seqeval`, the standard NER library), a prediction counts as correct **only if** the start boundary, end boundary, *and* entity type all match. Partial matches are counted as both FP and FN. This is stricter than token-level F1, and is what KTU expects you to use. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Viterbi Algorithm (KTU High-Yield) The Viterbi algorithm finds the most probable label sequence $Y^*$ for an observation sequence $X$ of length $n$. The naïve brute-force approach checks $L^n$ sequences (where $L$ is the number of tags), which is exponential. Viterbi uses dynamic programming to reduce this to $O(n \cdot L^2)$. ### Step 1 — Define the Recursion Let $\delta_t(j)$ be the **highest probability** of any path that ends in state $j$ at time $t$, having observed $X_1, \ldots, X_t$: $$\delta_t(j) = \max_{y_1, \ldots, y_{t-1}} \, P(y_1, \ldots, y_{t-1}, y_t = j, X_1, \ldots, X_t)$$ ### Step 2 — Base Case Initialization For $t = 1$: $$\delta_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(x_1)$$ where $\pi_j$ is the initial probability of state $j$, and $b_j(x_1)$ is the emission probability of observing $x_1$ in state $j$. ### Step 3 — Recursive Step (The Core Viterbi Update) By the Markov property and the chain rule, for $t \geq 2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_t(j) &= \max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(x_t) \\ \psi_t(j) &= \arg\max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning:** To reach state $j$ at time $t$, the best path must first reach *some* state $i$ at time $t-1$ and then transition $i \to j$. We pick the $i$ that maximizes this product, multiply by the emission of $x_t$ from $j$, and store both the score and the argmax. ### Step 4 — Termination $$P^* = \max_{j} \delta_n(j), \qquad y_n^* = \arg\max_{j} \delta_n(j)$$ ### Step 5 — Path Backtracking $$ \begin{aligned} y_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(y_{t+1}^*), \quad t = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 1 \end{aligned} $$ This yields the optimal tag sequence $Y^* = (y_1^*, y_2^*, \ldots, y_n^*)$. > [!NOTE] > **Worked Mini-Example (for intuition):** Suppose a 3-token sentence with 2 tags (P = PER, O = OUT). The Viterbi trellis fills in 6 cells $\delta_1(P), \delta_1(O), \delta_2(P), \delta_2(O), \delta_3(P), \delta_3(O)$. At $t=2$, computing $\delta_2(P) = \max(\delta_1(P) \cdot a_{P \to P}, \delta_1(O) \cdot a_{O \to P}) \cdot b_P(x_2)$. The argmax $\psi_2(P)$ remembers which previous state won, enabling backtracking. ## 3.2 Derivation of the BiLSTM-CRF Forward Pass The BiLSTM-CRF model computes an emission score matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times L}$ where $E_{t,j}$ is the score the BiLSTM assigns to tag $j$ at token $t$. The CRF layer then adds a transition matrix $T \in \mathbb{R}^{(L+2) \times (L+2)}$ (the +2 accounts for `START` and `STOP` sentinel tags). ### Step 1 — Define Path Score For any label sequence $Y = (y_1, \ldots, y_n)$, define: $$ \begin{aligned} S(X, Y) &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} E_{t, y_t} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} T_{y_{t-1}, y_t} \end{aligned} $$ Note: the second sum starts at $t=1$ where $y_0 = \text{START}$, and includes $T_{y_n, \text{STOP}}$. ### Step 2 — Apply Softmax over All Paths The probability of the gold path $Y_{\text{gold}}$ is: $$P(Y_{\text{gold}} \mid X) = \frac{\exp\!\big(S(X, Y_{\text{gold}})\big)}{\sum_{Y' \in \mathcal{Y}_X} \exp\!\big(S(X, Y')\big)} = \frac{\exp\!\big(S(X, Y_{\text{gold}})\big)}{Z(X)}$$ ### Step 3 — Define Training Loss (Negative Log-Likelihood) $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L} &= -\log P(Y_{\text{gold}} \mid X) \\ &= -S(X, Y_{\text{gold}}) + \log Z(X) \\ &= -S(X, Y_{\text{gold}}) + \log \sum_{Y'} \exp\!\big(S(X, Y')\big) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute $Z(X)$ via Forward Algorithm Let $\alpha_t(j)$ = total score of all paths ending at tag $j$ at time $t$. Recursion: $$ \begin{aligned} \alpha_1(j) &= E_{1,j} + T_{\text{START}, j} \\ \alpha_{t+1}(j) &= \log \sum_{i} \exp\!\big(\alpha_t(i) + T_{i,j}\big) + E_{t+1, j} \end{aligned} $$ Final log-partition: $$\log Z(X) = \log \sum_{j} \exp\!\big(\alpha_n(j) + T_{j, \text{STOP}}\big)$$ This is computed in $O(n \cdot L^2)$ — tractable for NER. ## 3.3 Confusion Matrix for NER Evaluation (Analytical) | | **Predicted ORG** | **Predicted PER** | **Predicted LOC** | **Predicted O** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Gold ORG** | TP | FP (boundary mismatch) | FN (wrong type) | FN | | **Gold PER** | FP (wrong type) | TP | FN | FN | | **Gold LOC** | FP | FN | TP | FN | | **Gold O** | FP | FP | FP | TN (background) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** "Background" tokens (tag `O`) are *not counted* in the F1 computation. seqeval skips them automatically. If you compute `sklearn.metrics.f1_score` naively, you will get an **inflated** score because the model trivially predicts `O` for most tokens. This is the #1 evaluation mistake students make in this lab. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Ambiguity-Resolution Walk-through > [!TIP] > **Classic Ambiguity:** *"Washington voted against the bill."* vs. *"Washington is the 42nd state."* The BiLSTM-CRF model processes token-by-token: 1. **Tokenize:** `[Washington, voted, against, the, bill, .]` 2. **Embed:** Map each token to a 768-dim vector (BERT) or 100-dim vector (GloVe). 3. **BiLSTM pass:** The hidden state at $t=1$ now encodes information from all 6 tokens in both directions. 4. **Emission score:** $E_{1, \text{PER}} = 2.1$, $E_{1, \text{LOC}} = 3.4$, $E_{1, \text{O}} = 0.5$. 5. **CRF transition check:** A `B-LOC` following `START` has transition score $T_{\text{START}, \text{B-LOC}} = 0.8$, but `B-PER` has $T_{\text{START}, \text{B-PER}} = 0.5$. 6. **Argmax path:** The total score for `B-LOC` wins → Washington is tagged as LOCATION. In the second sentence ("42nd state"), the BiLSTM's hidden state for "Washington" would have a much higher emission for `B-ORG` (because "state" is a strong organizational/contextual cue is absent; rather "42nd state" is a LOC-phrase). This **context-driven disambiguation** is the defining strength of BiLSTM-CRF. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End NER Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The complete NER system architecture is rendered below as a Mermaid block-level flow, showing data movement from raw text to evaluated F1-score. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Sentence Splitter] B --> C[Tokenizer
WordPiece or Whitespace] C --> D[Word Embedding Layer
BERT or GloVe] D --> E[Contextual Encoder
BiLSTM or Transformer] E --> F[Emission Score Matrix
n x L dimensions] F --> G[CRF Layer
Transition Constraints] G --> H[Tag Sequence
BIO labels] H --> I[Entity Span Extractor
B-I grouping] I --> J[seqeval Evaluator
P R F1] K[CoNLL-2003
Dataset] -.-> B L[Hyperparameters] -.-> E L -.-> G ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Solid arrows = data flow during training/inference. - Dashed arrows = configuration / side inputs. - The **CRF Layer** is the critical component that enforces valid BIO transitions and handles ambiguity. ## 4.2 BiLSTM-CRF Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputSeq["Input Token Sequence"] w1["x1: Apple"] w2["x2: announced"] w3["x3: iPhone"] end subgraph Embed["Embedding Layer"] e1["e1 300d"] e2["e2 300d"] e3["e3 300d"] end subgraph BiLSTM["Bidirectional LSTM"] f1["h-fwd-1"] f2["h-fwd-2"] f3["h-fwd-3"] b1["h-bwd-1"] b2["h-bwd-2"] b3["h-bwd-3"] end subgraph Concat["Concatenation"] h1["h1 = fwd1 + bwd1"] h2["h2 = fwd2 + bwd2"] h3["h3 = fwd3 + bwd3"] end subgraph CRFHead["CRF Decoding Layer"] emit["Emission Scores E"] trans["Transition Matrix T"] viterbi["Viterbi Decoder"] end subgraph Output["Output Tag Sequence"] y1["B-ORG"] y2["O"] y3["O"] end w1 --> e1 --> f1 w2 --> e2 --> f2 w3 --> e3 --> f3 w1 --> e1 --> b3 w2 --> e2 --> b2 w3 --> e3 --> b1 f1 --> h1 b1 --> h1 f2 --> h2 b2 --> h2 f3 --> h3 b3 --> h3 h1 --> emit h2 --> emit h3 --> emit trans --> viterbi emit --> viterbi viterbi --> y1 viterbi --> y2 viterbi --> y3 ``` ## 4.3 Ambiguity-Resolution Decision Flow This diagram shows how the model chooses between competing entity classes for a single ambiguous token. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["New Token: 'Apple'"]) --> Tok[Subword Tokenization] Tok --> Emb[BERT Embedding] Emb --> Enc[BERT Encoder
12 Layers] Enc --> Score{Compute Emission
Scores for All Tags} Score --> P1[Score B-ORG = 4.2] Score --> P2[Score B-MISC = 1.1] Score --> P3[Score O = 0.3] P1 --> CRF[Apply CRF Transition
Constraints] P2 --> CRF P3 --> CRF CRF --> Vit[Viterbi Decode] Vit --> Argmax{Find Argmax
over Tag Sequence} Argmax --> Out1["B-ORG
(chosen)"] Argmax --> Out2[Discard: B-MISC] Argmax --> Out3[Discard: O] Out1 --> Done(["Final Tag: B-ORG"]) ``` ## 4.4 State Diagram for BIO Tag Transitions (CRF Constraints) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> OUT OUT --> B_PER: B-PER (valid) OUT --> B_LOC: B-LOC (valid) OUT --> B_ORG: B-ORG (valid) OUT --> OUT: O (valid) B_PER --> I_PER: I-PER (valid) B_LOC --> I_LOC: I-LOC (valid) B_ORG --> I_ORG: I-ORG (valid) B_PER --> OUT: O (valid - entity ended) B_LOC --> OUT: O (valid - entity ended) B_ORG --> OUT: O (valid - entity ended) I_PER --> OUT: O (valid) I_PER --> B_LOC: B-LOC (valid - new entity) I_PER --> I_PER: I-PER (valid) I_PER --> B_PER: INVALID (forbidden) I_LOC --> I_PER: INVALID (forbidden) I_ORG --> I_LOC: INVALID (forbidden) B_PER --> B_PER: INVALID (no immediate duplicate) I_LOC --> B_LOC: INVALID ``` > [!NOTE] > The transitions marked **INVALID** in the diagram above are precisely the constraints that the CRF transition matrix $T$ enforces with very large negative scores (e.g., $T_{I\text{-}PER, B\text{-}PER} = -10000$). This is what makes the CRF layer so effective at handling **boundary ambiguity** in NER. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define Named Entity Recognition (NER). List any four standard entity types used in NER systems.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **NER** is a sequence labelling task in NLP that identifies and classifies named entities in text into predefined categories. The four standard entity types (as per the **CoNLL-2003** shared task) are: 1. **PER** (Person) — e.g., "Albert Einstein" 2. **LOC** (Location) — e.g., "Kerala" 3. **ORG** (Organization) — e.g., "Google" 4. **MISC** (Miscellaneous) — e.g., "Hindi" Other common types include DATE, TIME, MONEY, PERCENT, and FAC (facility). *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four types.)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Explain the BIO tagging scheme with a suitable example. Why is it preferred over a simple binary "entity / not-entity" tagging?** **Model Answer:** The **BIO scheme** tags each token in a sentence with one of three prefixes: - `B-XXX` — token begins an entity of type XXX. - `I-XXX` — token is inside (continuation of) an entity of type XXX. - `O` — token is outside any entity. **Example:** `"Marie Curie discovered radium."` - Token: `Marie` → `B-PER` - Token: `Curie` → `I-PER` - Token: `discovered` → `O` - Token: `radium` → `O` **Why preferred over binary tagging?** Binary tagging only tells us *which* tokens are entities, not *entity boundaries* or *types*. In multi-token entities like "Marie Curie" or "New York University", binary tagging cannot distinguish a single long entity from two adjacent short entities. BIO resolves this by marking the start (`B-`) and continuation (`I-`), enabling **boundary detection** as well as **classification**. *(3 marks: 1.5 for explanation, 1.5 for example and justification.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. You are required to attempt **ONE** of the two questions given. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain in detail the **BiLSTM-CRF architecture** for Named Entity Recognition. Clearly state how the BiLSTM layer captures contextual information and how the CRF layer enforces valid label transitions. Use a labelled diagram in your explanation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss at least **three major sources of ambiguity** in NER. For each source, explain how a BiLSTM-CRF model specifically resolves it. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — BiLSTM-CRF Architecture **Step 1 — Overall Architecture Overview:** The BiLSTM-CRF model is a two-stage neural architecture where: - **Stage 1 (BiLSTM):** Encodes each token into a context-aware vector representation. - **Stage 2 (CRF):** Decodes the sequence of vectors into a globally optimal label sequence. **Step 2 — Input and Embedding Layer:** Each input sentence $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is first converted into embeddings. In modern implementations, **pre-trained contextual embeddings** (BERT, RoBERTa) are used; in classical implementations, static embeddings (GloVe) + character-level CNN embeddings are concatenated. **Step 3 — Bidirectional LSTM Layer:** Two LSTMs process the sequence in opposite directions: $$ \begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{h_t} &= \text{LSTM}_\text{fwd}(x_t, \overrightarrow{h_{t-1}}) \\ \overleftarrow{h_t} &= \text{LSTM}_\text{bwd}(x_t, \overleftarrow{h_{t+1}}) \\ h_t &= [\overrightarrow{h_t} ; \overleftarrow{h_t}] \end{aligned} $$ The forward LSTM captures **past context** (tokens $x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}$), and the backward LSTM captures **future context** (tokens $x_{t+1}, \ldots, x_n$). Their concatenation $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}$ is the final contextual representation. **Step 4 — Emission Score Projection:** The BiLSTM output $h_t$ is linearly projected to a vector of size $L$ (the number of distinct tags), producing an **emission score matrix** $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times L}$ where $E_{t,j}$ is the score of tag $j$ at position $t$. **Step 5 — CRF Decoding Layer:** The CRF layer introduces a **transition matrix** $T \in \mathbb{R}^{(L+2) \times (L+2)}$ (with +2 for `START` and `STOP` sentinels). The score of a complete tag sequence $Y$ is: $$ S(X, Y) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} E_{t, y_t} + \sum_{t=0}^{n} T_{y_{t-1}, y_t} $$ During training, we minimize the negative log-likelihood: $$ \mathcal{L} = -S(X, Y_\text{gold}) + \log \sum_{Y'} \exp S(X, Y') $$ During inference, the **Viterbi algorithm** is used to find: $$ Y^* = \arg\max_{Y} S(X, Y) $$ **Step 6 — Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for a complete Mermaid diagram.) **Step 7 — Why This Architecture Works for NER:** - The BiLSTM supplies **rich contextual features** that disambiguate lexical items. - The CRF ensures **globally consistent** label sequences and prevents illegal transitions (e.g., `I-PER` after `O`). *(7 marks: 1 for overview, 2 for BiLSTM math, 2 for CRF math, 1 for diagram, 1 for justification.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b) — Three Major Sources of Ambiguity and Their Resolution **Ambiguity 1 — Lexical Ambiguity (Same word, different entity types):** *Example:* "Apple" → fruit vs. company. **Resolution by BiLSTM-CRF:** The BiLSTM reads the sentence bidirectionally. When the surrounding tokens are "iPhone" (backward direction) and "Tim Cook announced" (forward direction), the hidden state $h_t$ for "Apple" will have a high emission score for `B-ORG` and a low score for `O`. The CRF then picks the path that maximizes the global sequence score, choosing ORG. **Ambiguity 2 — Boundary Ambiguity (Where does the entity end?):** *Example:* "New York University" — one ORG vs. ORG + LOC. **Resolution by BiLSTM-CRF:** The CRF's transition matrix assigns a **high positive score** to `I-ORG → I-ORG` (continuing the entity) and a **high positive score** to `I-ORG → O` (ending the entity gracefully) — but the BiLSTM determines *which* transition is more likely given the word "University" (which is a strong signal to *end* the entity since it is a typical ORG-terminator). Without the CRF, the model could produce illegal sequences like `B-LOC, I-LOC, I-ORG` which the CRF layer forbids. **Ambiguity 3 — Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) Ambiguity (Unseen words during inference):** *Example:* A new startup name "Zynxora" never seen in training. **Resolution by BiLSTM-CRF:** When using **character-level embeddings** (a CNN or LSTM over characters), the model learns morphological patterns (capitalized initial, "ora" suffix, length). Combined with contextual cues from the BiLSTM, the model can confidently tag "Zynxora" as `B-ORG` even if it has never seen the exact word. BERT's subword tokenization provides an even more robust solution by breaking "Zynxora" into familiar subword units. *(7 marks: 2 per ambiguity + 1 for overall conclusion.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** With necessary equations, derive the **Viterbi algorithm** for finding the most probable tag sequence in an HMM-based NER system. State clearly the meaning of the back-pointer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **CRF-based NER model** mathematically. Show how the **partition function** is computed. Explain why CRF is preferred over HMM for NER. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Viterbi Derivation **Step 1 — Problem Statement:** Given an HMM with states (NER tags) $S = \{s_1, \ldots, s_L\}$ and an observation sequence $X = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$, find: $$ Y^* = \arg\max_{Y} P(Y \mid X) = \arg\max_{Y} P(X, Y) $$ **Step 2 — Why Brute Force Fails:** There are $L^n$ possible sequences; brute force is $O(L^n \cdot n)$ — exponential. **Step 3 — Recursion Definition:** Define $\delta_t(j)$ as the max probability of any path ending in state $j$ at time $t$: $$ \delta_t(j) = \max_{y_1, \ldots, y_{t-1}} P(y_1, \ldots, y_{t-1}, y_t = j, x_1, \ldots, x_t) $$ **Step 4 — Initialization ($t = 1$):** $$ \delta_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(x_1), \qquad \psi_1(j) = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Recursion ($t = 2, \ldots, n$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_t(j) &= \max_{i \in S} \big[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \big] \cdot b_j(x_t) \\ \psi_t(j) &= \arg\max_{i \in S} \big[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \big] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Termination:** $$ P^* = \max_{j \in S} \delta_n(j), \qquad y_n^* = \arg\max_{j \in S} \delta_n(j) $$ **Step 7 — Backtracking:** $$ y_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(y_{t+1}^*), \quad \text{for } t = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 1 $$ **Step 8 — Meaning of the Back-Pointer $\psi_t(j)$:** The back-pointer $\psi_t(j)$ stores the *identity of the previous state* $i$ that maximized the recursion at time $t$ for state $j$. This is the memory that allows Viterbi to recover the **entire best path** after the forward pass is complete. Without the back-pointer, we would only know the *score* of the best path, not the *path itself*. **Complexity:** $O(n \cdot L^2)$ — linear in sentence length, quadratic in tag set size. *(7 marks: 1 for problem statement, 2 for initialization, 2 for recursion, 1 for termination, 1 for back-pointer explanation.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b) — CRF-based NER **Step 1 — CRF Definition:** A linear-chain CRF defines the conditional probability of a label sequence $Y$ given input $X$ as: $$ P(Y \mid X) = \frac{1}{Z(X)} \exp\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y_{t-1}, y_t, X, t) \right) $$ Where $f_k$ are **feature functions** that can depend on the entire input $X$, the current position $t$, and the previous/current labels. For NER, typical features include: - Word identity, capitalization, suffix/prefix. - Previous and current tags (transition feature). - Word shape, presence in a gazetteer. **Step 2 — Partition Function:** The partition function $Z(X)$ normalizes the probability by summing over **all possible** label sequences: $$ Z(X) = \sum_{Y'} \exp\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y'_{t-1}, y'_t, X, t) \right) $$ This is computed in $O(n \cdot L^2)$ using the **forward algorithm** (analogous to HMM): $$ \alpha_1(j) = \sum_{k} \lambda_k f_k(\text{START}, j, X, 1) $$ $$ \alpha_t(j) = \log \sum_{i} \exp\!\big( \alpha_{t-1}(i) + \sum_k \lambda_k f_k(i, j, X, t) \big) $$ $$ \log Z(X) = \log \sum_{j} \exp\!\big( \alpha_n(j) \big) $$ **Step 3 — Why CRF is Preferred over HMM for NER:** | Property | HMM | CRF | |----------|-----|-----| | **Model type** | Generative $P(X, Y)$ | Discriminative $P(Y \mid X)$ | | **Feature richness** | Limited to emission $P(x_t \mid y_t)$ | Arbitrary overlapping features | | **Independence assumption** | Strong (observations independent given state) | None (can use any global feature) | | **Handles rich word features?** | No (must pre-tabulate emissions) | Yes (capitalization, suffix, etc.) | | **Avoids label bias?** | N/A | Yes (global normalization) | | **Performance on CoNLL-2003** | F1 ≈ 80–85% | F1 ≈ 90–94% | **Key reason:** HMMs must *generate* the observations, forcing strong independence assumptions that limit feature engineering. CRFs only model the *boundary* between $X$ and $Y$, allowing them to use rich, overlapping, non-independent features — a critical advantage for NER where capitalization, suffixes, and gazetteer matches are all informative. *(7 marks: 2 for CRF definition, 2 for partition function, 3 for the comparison table.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** confuse Viterbi's `argmax` with the forward algorithm's `sum` — Viterbi uses `max`, forward uses `logsumexp`. Mixing these up is an automatic 4-mark deduction. > 2. **Always** state the **back-pointer** $\psi_t(j)$ explicitly. The Viterbi algorithm is incomplete without it. > 3. **CRF vs HMM:** Students often write "CRF is better because it uses deep learning" — this is **false**. CRF is a *discriminative graphical model*, not a neural network. The BiLSTM-CRF is what adds deep learning. Do not conflate the two. > 4. **Evaluation:** Always specify the library used (`seqeval`) and the evaluation mode (entity-level strict). A vague "the model achieved 95% accuracy" without context loses 3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2 (NER Sequence Labelling)** - [x] **NER is a sequence labelling task**, mapping $X = (x_1, \ldots, x_n) \to Y = (y_1, \ldots, y_n)$. - [x] **BIO tagging** is the default scheme; `B-` = beginning, `I-` = inside, `O` = outside. - [x] **HMM** is the generative baseline using transition $a_{ij}$ and emission $b_j(x_t)$ probabilities. - [x] **Viterbi algorithm** uses dynamic programming: $\delta_t(j) = \max_i [\delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}] \cdot b_j(x_t)$ with back-pointer $\psi_t(j)$. - [x] **CRF** is the discriminative model: $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{\exp(\sum \lambda_k f_k)}{Z(X)}$ — uses **global normalization**. - [x] **BiLSTM** captures bidirectional context: $h_t = [\overrightarrow{h_t} ; \overleftarrow{h_t}]$. - [x] **BiLSTM-CRF** is the modern workhorse — BiLSTM for embeddings, CRF for sequence decoding. - [x] **BERT-based NER** uses Transformer encoders + CRF head; best for OOV and contextual ambiguity. - [x] **Ambiguity types** to memorize: lexical, boundary, OOV, long-range, nested, discourse. - [x] **Evaluation metrics** are **entity-level** P, R, F1 — *not* token-level. Use `seqeval` library. - [x] **Strict evaluation** requires exact match of (start, end, type) — partial matches count as both FP and FN. - [x] **CoNLL-2003** is the standard benchmark; tags = {PER, LOC, ORG, MISC}. - [x] **F1 formula:** $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$. - [x] **CRF advantage over HMM:** arbitrary overlapping features + global normalization + no label bias. - [x] **Always remember** the partition function $Z(X)$ in the CRF formula; omitting it loses marks. - [x] **KTU expects Python code** in this lab (sklearn-crfsuite, spaCy, or PyTorch) — this is a CS lab, not a theory paper.

topics.title: Build a general Information Extraction (IE) pipeline, focusing on extracting structured information such as names, dates, and relations from unstructured text
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCML607 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Build a general Information Extraction (IE) pipeline, focusing on extracting structured information such as names, dates, and relations from unstructured text

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Information Extraction (IE)** is a sub-field of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Computational Linguistics that focuses on the automatic extraction of structured, machine-readable information from unstructured or semi-structured natural language text. An **IE Pipeline** is a sequential architectural composition of NLP modules — typically including *Sentence Segmentation*, *Tokenization*, *Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging*, *Named Entity Recognition (NER)*, *Relation Extraction (RE)*, and *Coreference Resolution* — that collectively transform raw text into a structured representation (e.g., tuples, knowledge graphs, JSON objects).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCML607 | Module 2):** Students are expected to **implement a working end-to-end IE pipeline** in Python using established libraries such as `spaCy`, `NLTK`, or `Stanford CoreNLP`, and validate the output against a sample text corpus.

---

## 1.2 The Four Classical Sub-Tasks of Information Extraction

| Sub-Task | What It Extracts | Example Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Real-world objects (Persons, Organizations, Locations, Dates, Money) | `[Barack Obama]` → PERSON |
| **Relation Extraction (RE)** | Semantic relations between entities | `(Obama, born_in, Hawaii)` |
| **Event Extraction** | Events and their participants | `(Acquisition, Acquirer=Google, Target=Fitbit)` |
| **Coreference Resolution** | Mentions referring to the same entity | `["Obama", "he", "the former president"]` → Entity #1 |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Clipper

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you have a 50-page newspaper written entirely in prose. Your job is to fill a database table with `Date | Event | Person Involved | Location`. 
> 
> - **Reading the article** = Tokenization + POS Tagging
> - **Highlighting names, places, and dates with a yellow marker** = Named Entity Recognition
> - **Drawing arrows between markers to show "X happened at Y"** = Relation Extraction
> - **Replacing "he" or "the company" with the actual name** = Coreference Resolution
> 
> An **IE Pipeline** is simply a robot assistant that performs all four of these tasks automatically, page after page, in milliseconds.

---

## 1.4 Engineering Metrics for IE Systems (Bolded Per Protocol)

- **Precision (P):** Fraction of extracted entities that are *correct*. Formula: $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall (R):** Fraction of *correct* entities that were extracted. Formula: $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Formula: $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$
- **Throughput:** Sentences processed per second (typical spaCy benchmark: **~10,000 sentences/sec** on a single CPU core).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard IE Pipeline (Layered Architecture)

A production-grade IE pipeline is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** of NLP components. Each layer consumes the output of the previous layer and enriches it with linguistic annotations.

### Layer 1 — Text Acquisition & Sentence Segmentation
- The raw document is split into sentences using punctuation rules, capitalisation heuristics, or statistical models.
- **Why?** Most downstream taggers operate per-sentence; cross-sentence dependencies are handled by coreference models.

### Layer 2 — Tokenization
- Sentences are split into atomic units (tokens): words, numbers, punctuation, sub-word units.
- spaCy's tokenizer handles whitespace, prefix/suffix/infix splitting, and special cases (e.g., `U.S.A.`).

### Layer 3 — Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging
- Each token is assigned a grammatical category (NOUN, VERB, ADJ, etc.).
- **Why?** POS tags act as a *feature* for the NER model — proper nouns are strong indicators of entities.

### Layer 4 — Named Entity Recognition (NER)
- The core IE layer. A sequence-labeling model (typically a **BiLSTM-CRF**, **Transformer**, or **CNN-BiLSTM-CRF**) assigns an entity label to each token.
- Default spaCy `en_core_web_sm` model recognizes **18 entity types**: PERSON, NORP, FAC, ORG, GPE, LOC, PRODUCT, EVENT, WORK_OF_ART, LAW, LANGUAGE, DATE, TIME, MONEY, PERCENT, QUANTITY, ORDINAL, CARDINAL.

### Layer 5 — Relation Extraction (RE)
- Identifies semantic relationships between detected entities. Common paradigms:
  - **Rule-based:** Dependency parse tree patterns (e.g., `PERSON --nsubj--> born --prep_in--> GPE`).
  - **Supervised ML:** SVM/Neural classifiers over lexical + syntactic features.
  - **Transformer-based:** Models like `bert-base-ner-re` or `REBEL`.

### Layer 6 — Coreference Resolution
- Clusters mentions that refer to the same real-world entity.
- spaCy 3.x uses the `coreferee` extension or external `neuralcoref` pipelines.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (not `|`) when writing absolute value or cardinality inside markdown tables to preserve table syntax integrity.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Measures exactness of extraction |
| **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Measures coverage of extraction |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced single metric |
| **Macro-F1** | $F_{macro} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{1,i}$ | Average over all entity types |
| **NER as Sequence Labeling** | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(y \vert x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ | Maximize label sequence probability |
| **BIO Tagging Scheme** | `B-X`, `I-X`, `O` | Beginning, Inside, Outside tags |
| **IOBES Tagging** | `B`, `I`, `O`, `E`, `S` | More expressive than BIO |
| **Pipeline Latency** | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$ | Sum of per-component time |
| **Dependency Triplet** | $(h, r, t)$ | Head, Relation, Tail — knowledge graph form |
| **Cardinality of Entities** | $\vert E \vert$ | Number of unique entities in document |
| **Entity Density** | $\rho = \frac{\vert E \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Entities per sentence |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Extracting drug names, dosages, and adverse events from clinical notes (used in **IBM Watson Health**, **Google Health**).
- **Finance:** Parsing earnings reports, SEC filings, and news to populate knowledge graphs for algorithmic trading.
- **Legal Tech:** Extracting clauses, parties, and dates from contracts (e.g., **Evisort**, **Luminance**).
- **Cybersecurity:** Extracting Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) — IP addresses, hashes, CVEs — from threat-intelligence reports.
- **Search Engines:** Google's Knowledge Panel is powered by an IE pipeline over the entire web crawl.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough

## 3.1 Lab Objective (As per KTU PCCML607 Record)

> Build a Python-based Information Extraction pipeline that takes unstructured English text as input and returns structured information including: (i) all named entities with their types, (ii) all detected dates, (iii) all relational tuples between entities.

## 3.2 Software & Hardware Requirements

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Language | Python 3.8+ |
| Primary Library | `spaCy 3.x` |
| Model | `en_core_web_sm` (or `en_core_web_trf` for transformer-based) |
| Supporting Libraries | `pandas`, `networkx`, `matplotlib` |
| IDE | Jupyter Notebook / VS Code / Google Colab |
| Hardware | Minimum 4 GB RAM, 2 GB free disk for model |

## 3.3 Installation Steps (Write This in Your Lab Record)

```bash
# Step 1: Create a virtual environment
python -m venv ie_lab_env
source ie_lab_env/bin/activate          # Linux/Mac
ie_lab_env\Scripts\activate            # Windows

# Step 2: Install spaCy and the English model
pip install spacy pandas networkx matplotlib
python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm

# Step 3: Verify installation
python -c "import spacy; nlp = spacy.load('en_core_web_sm'); print('OK')"
```

## 3.4 Algorithm — Step-by-Step Pipeline Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The following pseudocode and Python implementation is the **core deliverable** for the KTU lab record. Each step corresponds to a cell in your Jupyter Notebook.

### Step 1 — Load the Pipeline

```python
import spacy
from spacy import displacy
import pandas as pd
import networkx as nx
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load the pre-trained English model
nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")
print("Pipeline components:", nlp.pipe_names)
# Expected Output: ['tok2vec', 'tagger', 'parser', 'attribute_ruler', 'lemmatizer', 'ner']
```

### Step 2 — Define the Input Corpus

```python
corpus = """
Apple Inc. was founded by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne on April 1, 1976,
in Cupertino, California. The company is headquartered at Apple Park, which opened in
2017. Tim Cook became CEO of Apple in August 2011, succeeding Steve Jobs who passed
away on October 5, 2011. In 2022, Apple reported revenue of $394.3 billion, with
its iPhone contributing over 50% of total sales. The company employs approximately
164,000 people worldwide and was recently valued at $3 trillion by market analysts
in New York.
"""
```

### Step 3 — Run the Pipeline

```python
doc = nlp(corpus)

# Inspect each token with its linguistic annotations
for token in doc[:20]:
    print(f"{token.text:15s} | POS: {token.pos_:8s} | DEP: {token.dep_:12s} | HEAD: {token.head.text}")
```

### Step 4 — Extract Named Entities (NER Step)

```python
entities_data = []
for ent in doc.ents:
    entities_data.append({
        "Entity": ent.text,
        "Type": ent.label_,
        "Start_Char": ent.start_char,
        "End_Char": ent.end_char,
        "Sentence_ID": ent.sent.start
    })

df_entities = pd.DataFrame(entities_data)
print(df_entities)
```

### Step 5 — Extract All Dates (Focused Sub-Task)

```python
dates_extracted = [ent.text for ent in doc.ents if ent.label_ == "DATE"]
print("Dates Found:", dates_extracted)
# Expected: ['April 1, 1976', '2017', 'August 2011', 'October 5, 2011', '2022']
```

### Step 6 — Extract Relations Using Dependency Parse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **highest-scoring part** of the experiment. We extract `(Subject, Relation, Object)` triplets using the dependency tree.

```python
def extract_relations(doc):
    relations = []
    for token in doc:
        if token.dep_ == "nsubj" and token.head.pos_ == "VERB":
            subject = token.text
            verb = token.head.text
            # Find the direct object (dobj) or prepositional object (pobj)
            objects = [child for child in token.head.children 
                       if child.dep_ in ("dobj", "attr", "pobj")]
            for obj in objects:
                # Walk up prepositions if present
                full_object = obj.text
                prep = [c for c in obj.children if c.dep_ == "prep"]
                relations.append((subject, verb, full_object))
    return relations

relations = extract_relations(doc)
for r in relations:
    print(f"({r[0]}, {r[1]}, {r[2]})")
```

### Step 7 — Visualize the Entity Knowledge Graph

```python
G = nx.DiGraph()

# Add entity nodes colored by type
color_map = {"PERSON": "lightblue", "ORG": "lightgreen", "GPE": "salmon",
             "DATE": "gold", "MONEY": "plum"}

for ent in doc.ents:
    G.add_node(ent.text, label=ent.label_)

# Add edges for co-occurrence in same sentence
for sent in doc.sents:
    sent_ents = [e for e in doc.ents if e.start >= sent.start and e.end <= sent.end]
    for i in range(len(sent_ents)):
        for j in range(i+1, len(sent_ents)):
            G.add_edge(sent_ents[i].text, sent_ents[j].text)

pos = nx.spring_layout(G, k=0.8, seed=42)
colors = [color_map.get(G.nodes[n].get("label",""), "gray") for n in G.nodes]
nx.draw(G, pos, with_labels=True, node_color=colors, 
        node_size=2000, font_size=8, arrows=True)
plt.title("Knowledge Graph Extracted from Text")
plt.show()
```

## 3.5 Expected Output (Sample Lab Record Table)

| Entity Text | Entity Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Apple Inc. | ORG | Organization |
| Steve Jobs | PERSON | Person |
| Steve Wozniak | PERSON | Person |
| April 1, 1976 | DATE | Date |
| Cupertino | GPE | Geopolitical Entity |
| California | GPE | Geopolitical Entity |
| Tim Cook | PERSON | Person |
| $394.3 billion | MONEY | Monetary Value |
| 164,000 | CARDINAL | Number |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IE Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Unstructured Text] --> B[Sentence Segmenter]
    B --> C[Tokenizer]
    C --> D[POS Tagger]
    D --> E[Dependency Parser]
    D --> F[NER Model]
    E --> G[Relation Extractor]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Coreference Resolver]
    H --> I[Structured Output JSON Table or Knowledge Graph]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Data-Flow Architecture (Block-Level Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        In1[Document 1 Text File]
        In2[Document 2 News Article]
        In3[Document 3 Web Scraped]
    end
    
    InputStage --> PreProc[Preprocessing Module]
    PreProc -->|Cleaned Text| PipeCore
    
    subgraph PipeCore[spaCy Pipeline Core]
        S1[Sentencizer] --> S2[Tokenizer]
        S2 --> S3[Tagger POS]
        S2 --> S4[Parser Dep]
        S3 --> S5[NER Component]
        S4 --> S5
        S5 --> S6[Lemma Normalizer]
    end
    
    PipeCore -->|Doc Object| PostProc[Postprocessing Module]
    
    PostProc --> Out1[Entity List DataFrame]
    PostProc --> Out2[Date List]
    PostProc --> Out3[Relation Triplets]
    PostProc --> Out4[Knowledge Graph NetworkX]
    PostProc --> Out5[JSON Structured Record]
```

## 4.3 Entity Type Taxonomy (spaCy `en_core_web_sm`)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Named Entity Types 18 Classes]
    Root --> P1[PERSON]
    Root --> P2[ORG Organization]
    Root --> P3[GPE Geopolitical]
    Root --> P4[LOC Location]
    Root --> P5[DATE]
    Root --> P6[TIME]
    Root --> P7[MONEY]
    Root --> P8[PERCENT]
    Root --> P9[PRODUCT]
    Root --> P10[EVENT]
    Root --> P11[WORK_OF_ART]
    Root --> P12[LAW]
    Root --> P13[LANGUAGE]
    Root --> P14[QUANTITY]
    Root --> P15[ORDINAL]
    Root --> P16[CARDINAL]
    Root --> P17[FAC Facility]
    Root --> P18[NORP Nationality]
```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Pipeline Execution Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant P as Python Script
    participant M as spaCy Model
    participant O as Output Handler
    
    U->>P: Provide raw text
    P->>M: nlp(text) call
    M->>M: Tokenize
    M->>M: POS Tag
    M->>M: Parse Dependencies
    M->>M: Run NER
    M-->>P: Return Doc object
    P->>P: Iterate doc.ents
    P->>O: Write DataFrame
    P->>O: Save JSON file
    P->>O: Render Knowledge Graph
    O-->>U: Display structured results
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Information Extraction (IE) and list its four classical sub-tasks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Information Extraction (IE) is the NLP task of automatically retrieving **structured information** such as entities, relations, events, and coreferences from **unstructured natural-language text**. Its four classical sub-tasks are:
1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — identifies and classifies named entities.
2. **Relation Extraction (RE)** — detects semantic relations between entities.
3. **Event Extraction** — identifies events with their participants and attributes.
4. **Coreference Resolution** — clusters all mentions referring to the same real-world entity.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing + 1 mark for correct categorization. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the BIO tagging scheme with an example used in NER.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **BIO (Begin, Inside, Outside)** scheme is a token-level labeling format for sequence labeling tasks like NER.
- **B-X** → *Begin* of an entity of type X.
- **I-X** → *Inside* (continuation) of an entity of type X.
- **O** → *Outside* any entity (regular token).

**Example for the sentence "Barack Obama visited Paris":**

| Token | BIO Tag |
|---|---|
| Barack | B-PER |
| Obama | I-PER |
| visited | O |
| Paris | B-LOC |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for acronym expansion, 1 mark for example. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly **forget to install the spaCy model separately** (`python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm`) and lose **2 marks** for a failed import. Additionally, examiners deduct marks if you **hard-code outputs** instead of writing a **generalized function** that accepts any input text. Always wrap your logic inside a `def extract_information(text):` function.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q-A. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a general Information Extraction pipeline. Mention the role of each component.

**Model Solution:**

An IE pipeline is a **sequential composition of NLP modules** that incrementally enriches the linguistic representation of text. The standard architecture comprises:

1. **Sentence Segmenter:** Splits the document into sentences using punctuation and capitalization heuristics. (1 mark)
2. **Tokenizer:** Decomposes each sentence into atomic tokens (words, numbers, punctuation). Handles edge cases like contractions, hyphenated words, and abbreviations. (1 mark)
3. **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagger:** Assigns grammatical categories (NOUN, VERB, ADJ) using statistical models (e.g., averaged perceptron, transformer). (1 mark)
4. **Dependency Parser:** Builds a syntactic tree showing grammatical relations between tokens (e.g., `nsubj`, `dobj`, `prep_in`). (1 mark)
5. **Named Entity Recognizer (NER):** A sequence-labeling model (BiLSTM-CRF or Transformer) that labels tokens with entity types such as PERSON, ORG, GPE, DATE. (1 mark)
6. **Relation Extractor:** Identifies semantic relationships between detected entities using rule-based dependency patterns or neural classifiers. (1 mark)
7. **Coreference Resolver:** Clusters pronominal and nominal mentions that refer to the same entity. (1 mark)

**Block Diagram (Reference Section 4.1):** Award **1 mark** for the correct diagram showing data flow from raw text → structured output.

---

**Q-A. (b) [7 Marks]** Write a Python program using `spaCy` to extract all named entities and dates from the given text: *"Microsoft was founded by Bill Gates on April 4, 1975, in Albuquerque. Satya Nadella became CEO in 2014."* Display the output in tabular form.

**Model Solution:**

```python
import spacy
import pandas as pd

# Step 1: Load model
nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")

# Step 2: Define input text
text = ("Microsoft was founded by Bill Gates on April 4, 1975, in Albuquerque. "
        "Satya Nadella became CEO in 2014.")

# Step 3: Process text
doc = nlp(text)

# Step 4: Build entity table (2 marks for correct logic)
data = [{"Entity": ent.text, "Type": ent.label_, "Description": spacy.explain(ent.label_)} 
        for ent in doc.ents]

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df)
```

**Expected Output Table (2 marks for correct output):**

| Entity | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Microsoft | ORG | Companies, agencies, institutions |
| Bill Gates | PERSON | People, including fictional |
| April 4, 1975 | DATE | Absolute or relative dates or periods |
| Albuquerque | GPE | Countries, cities, states |
| Satya Nadella | PERSON | People, including fictional |
| 2014 | DATE | Absolute or relative dates or periods |

**Bonus (1 mark):** For filtering only dates:
```python
dates = [ent.text for ent in doc.ents if ent.label_ == "DATE"]
print("Extracted Dates:", dates)
# Output: ['April 4, 1975', '2014']
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q-B. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Why is F1 preferred over accuracy for evaluating IE systems?

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Precision (P):** Of all entities the system *predicted*, how many were correct. $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall (R):** Of all entities that *actually exist*, how many did the system find. $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean that balances P and R. $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**
Suppose a system finds **70 entities**, of which **60 are correct** (TP = 60, FP = 10), but the gold standard has **100 entities** (FN = 40).
- $P = 60 / 70 = 0.857$ (85.7%)
- $R = 60 / 100 = 0.60$ (60%)
- $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.857 \times 0.60}{0.857 + 0.60} = 0.706$ (70.6%)

**Why F1 is preferred over Accuracy for IE (2 marks):**
- IE tasks suffer from **class imbalance** — most tokens are *not* entities. A trivial system that labels every token as `O` (Outside) achieves >95% accuracy but 0% F1.
- F1 penalizes both false positives and false negatives equally.
- F1 is the **standard metric** in the CoNLL-2003 shared task for NER evaluation.

---

**Q-B. (b) [7 Marks]** Write a Python function `extract_relations(text)` that uses spaCy's dependency parser to extract `(subject, verb, object)` triplets. Demonstrate it on the sample text: *"Google acquired YouTube in 2006. Sundar Pichai leads Google."*

**Model Solution:**

```python
import spacy

nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")

def extract_relations(text):
    doc = nlp(text)
    triplets = []
    for token in doc:
        # Identify subject-verb-object patterns
        if token.dep_ == "nsubj" and token.head.pos_ == "VERB":
            subject = token.text
            verb = token.head.lemma_   # use lemma for normalized form
            # Find object dependencies under the head verb
            for child in token.head.children:
                if child.dep_ in ("dobj", "attr", "pobj"):
                    triplets.append((subject, verb, child.text))
    return triplets

# Demonstration
sample = "Google acquired YouTube in 2006. Sundar Pichai leads Google."
print(extract_relations(sample))
```

**Expected Output (2 marks for correct triplets):**
```
[('Google', 'acquire', 'YouTube'), ('Sundar', 'lead', 'Google')]
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks total):**
- Correct spaCy import & model load: **1 mark**
- Correct loop over `doc` with dependency check: **2 marks**
- Correct handling of `nsubj` + `dobj/attr/pobj`: **2 marks**
- Correct expected output: **1 mark**
- Use of `lemma_` (best practice) bonus: **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Read This Before Your Lab Exam**

- [ ] **IE Pipeline = NER + RE + Coref + Event Extraction**, all operating on tokenized, POS-tagged text.
- [ ] **spaCy** is the de-facto library for KTU lab experiments. Always run `python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm` after `pip install spacy`.
- [ ] **NER model** in spaCy uses a **CNN + transition-based parser** (not just a CRF); be ready to explain this in viva.
- [ ] **18 entity types** in `en_core_web_sm`: PERSON, NORP, FAC, ORG, GPE, LOC, PRODUCT, EVENT, WORK_OF_ART, LAW, LANGUAGE, DATE, TIME, MONEY, PERCENT, QUANTITY, ORDINAL, CARDINAL.
- [ ] **BIO tagging** is the standard labeling format; **IOBES** is an enhanced version used in some papers.
- [ ] **Precision, Recall, F1** are evaluated per entity type, then macro-averaged.
- [ ] **Relation Extraction** can be rule-based (dependency patterns), feature-based ML, or transformer-based (e.g., `REBEL`).
- [ ] Always wrap your code in **reusable functions** (`def extract_info(text):`) — examiners prefer generalized solutions.
- [ ] **Coreference Resolution** is the most computationally expensive step; spaCy needs the `coreferee` extension.
- [ ] **Output formats** expected: `DataFrame` for entities, list of `tuples` for relations, `JSON` for structured records, `NetworkX` graph for visualization.
- [ ] **Common Viva Question:** *"Why can't we just use a regular expression to extract entities?"* → Answer: Regex is brittle, cannot handle context, ambiguity, or novel entity names. ML/NER models generalize.
- [ ] **Common Viva Question:** *"What is the difference between NER and POS tagging?"* → Answer: POS tags *all* tokens with grammatical class; NER labels *only entity mentions* with semantic categories like PERSON, ORG.
- [ ] **File Submission:** Lab record must include aim, algorithm, code, output screenshots, and result interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement supervised learning approaches to relation detection and classification, and apply them to extract relationships between entities in a text corpus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCML607 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement supervised learning approaches to relation detection and classification, and apply them to extract relationships between entities in a text corpus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Relation Detection and Classification (RDC)** is a Natural Language Processing (NLP) sub-task under Information Extraction that aims to identify semantic relationships between pairs of predefined entities (typically Named Entities such as PERSON, ORGANIZATION, LOCATION) in unstructured or semi-structured text. In the **supervised learning paradigm**, the system is trained on a labeled corpus where each candidate entity pair is annotated with a relationship type (e.g., *CEO_of*, *located_in*, *founded_by*) drawn from a closed relation schema. The model learns a decision function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ that maps an input feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (derived from a sentence containing an entity pair) to a discrete relation label $y \in \mathcal{R}$, where $\mathcal{R}$ is the set of predefined relations plus the negative class `NO_RELATION`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "relation detection" refers to determining *whether* a relation exists, while "relation classification" refers to assigning the correct *type* of relation. In KTU lab experiments, both tasks are typically performed jointly as a multi-class classification problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **newspaper editor** building an organized knowledge base from raw news articles. As you read each sentence, you must:
1. **Spot the main characters** (entities) — e.g., *"Sundar Pichai"* and *"Google"*.
2. **Detect whether they are connected** (relation detection) — yes, they are related.
3. **Classify the type of connection** (relation classification) — this is a *"CEO_of"* relationship.

A **supervised learning model** is like a trainee editor who has previously studied **thousands of pre-edited articles** (the labeled training corpus) where the relationships have already been highlighted. By observing patterns (who appears where, what verbs connect them, the distance between their names), the trainee learns to predict relationships in **new, unseen articles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Powering knowledge graphs (Google Knowledge Panel, Wikidata), biomedical databases (drug-protein interactions in PubMed), financial analytics (company-acquisition events from news), and question-answering systems.

## 1.3 Core Components of a Supervised Relation Extraction System

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Entity Pair Identification** | Locate candidate (e1, e2) pairs in a sentence | (Sundar Pichai, Google) |
| **Feature Extractor** | Converts sentence context into numeric vector | Lexical, syntactic, positional features |
| **Classifier** | Supervised model that learns $f(x) = y$ | SVM, Logistic Regression, Neural Network |
| **Label Set $\mathcal{R}$** | Predefined relation types | {CEO_of, located_in, founded_by, NO_RELATION} |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Supervised Learning Pipeline for Relation Extraction

The end-to-end pipeline consists of five well-defined stages. Each stage's correctness directly determines the final F1 score on the KTU lab evaluation rubric.

### Stage 1 — Named Entity Recognition (NER) Preprocessing
- Entities must be pre-identified and typed (e.g., PER, ORG, LOC).
- The KTU lab typically assumes entities are already tagged using a regex or pre-trained model such as **spaCy's `en_core_web_sm`**.

### Stage 2 — Candidate Pair Generation
- For a sentence with $n$ entities, generate ordered or unordered pairs.
- Number of candidate pairs: $C(n, 2) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ (unordered).
- Each pair becomes a potential **instance** for the classifier.

### Stage 3 — Feature Engineering
- **Lexical features:** entity types, entity tokens, surrounding words.
- **Positional features:** distance in tokens between the two entities.
- **Syntactic features:** dependency path between entities (e.g., *nsubj*, *dobj*).
- **Semantic features:** WordNet hypernyms, embedding similarity $\cos(\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2)$.

### Stage 4 — Supervised Model Training
- Train a classifier on the feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ and label vector $y \in \{1, \ldots, K\}^N$, where $N$ is the number of training instances and $K = \vert \mathcal{R} \vert$ is the number of relation types.

### Stage 5 — Prediction & Evaluation
- Predict on held-out test set and compute **Precision**, **Recall**, and **F1-score**.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

### 2.2.1 Binary and Multi-class Classification

For a single instance $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with label $y_i \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$:

The **softmax function** converts raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution over the $K$ relation classes:

$$
P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x} + b_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(\mathbf{w}_j^\top \mathbf{x} + b_j)}
$$

### 2.2.2 Cross-Entropy Loss (Objective Function)

The model parameters $\Theta = \{\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{b}\}$ are learned by minimizing the **categorical cross-entropy loss**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\Theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\} \cdot \log P(y_i = k \mid \mathbf{x}_i; \Theta)
$$

where $\mathbb{1}\{\cdot\}$ is the indicator function.

### 2.2.3 Decision Rule

The predicted class for instance $\mathbf{x}_i$ is:

$$
\hat{y}_i = \underset{k \in \mathcal{R}}{\arg\max} \; P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}_i; \Theta)
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value / cardinality inside tables to avoid markdown breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of predicted relations that are correct | $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of true relations that are recovered | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | $C(n,2) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Candidate entity pairs per sentence | Integer |
| 5 | $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N}$ | Overall correctness | $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | $P(k) = \frac{\exp(z_k)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j)}$ | Softmax probability for class $k$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 7 | $\cos(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\mathbf{u}^\top \mathbf{v}}{\vert \mathbf{u} \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{v} \vert}$ | Cosine similarity of two entity embeddings | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 8 | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Final relation prediction | $\mathcal{R}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical NLP:** Extracting *drug-gene-mutation* interactions from PubMed abstracts to build curated databases such as **PharmGKB** and **STRING**.
- **Financial intelligence:** Detecting *acquisition*, *merger*, and *investment* relations from Reuters/Bloomberg news feeds.
- **Cybersecurity:** Identifying *threat-actor-target* relationships in CTI (Cyber Threat Intelligence) reports.
- **E-commerce knowledge graphs:** Linking *brand*, *product*, and *category* entities for semantic search at Amazon/Flipkart scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Softmax Cross-Entropy Gradient

The goal is to show how a single training update lowers the loss when the model misclassifies an instance. This is the **why** behind backpropagation in the lab's logistic-regression-style classifier.

### Step 1 — Define the linear score

For input feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the score for class $k$ is:

$$
z_k = \mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b_k
$$

### Step 2 — Convert scores to probabilities via softmax

$$
p_k = \frac{\exp(z_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(z_j)}
$$

### Step 3 — Write the cross-entropy for one instance

For the true class $y_i = c$:

$$
\ell_i = -\log p_c = -\log \left( \frac{\exp(z_c)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(z_j)} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Expand using logarithm properties

$$
\ell_i = -z_c + \log \left( \sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(z_j) \right)
$$

This is the **log-sum-exp** form, which is numerically stable when implemented with the standard `logsumexp` trick.

### Step 5 — Compute gradient w.r.t. $z_k$

Using the derivative of the log-sum-exp term:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial z_k} = p_k - \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\}
$$

This elegant result says: **the gradient is the difference between the predicted probability and the ground-truth one-hot indicator**.

### Step 6 — Chain rule to weights

$$
\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial \mathbf{w}_k} = (p_k - \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\}) \cdot \mathbf{x}_i
$$

### Step 7 — Apply gradient descent update with learning rate $\eta$

$$
\mathbf{w}_k \leftarrow \mathbf{w}_k - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (p_k^{(i)} - \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\}) \cdot \mathbf{x}_i
$$

**Intuition:** If $p_k$ is too high for a wrong class, $(p_k - 0)$ is positive, so the weight is *decreased* — pushing the model away from the wrong answer.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Computing Precision/Recall/F1

Suppose a KTU lab test set contains the following predictions for the relation class *CEO_of*:

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| True Positives ($TP$) | 30 |
| False Positives ($FP$) | 10 |
| False Negatives ($FN$) | 20 |
| True Negatives ($TN$) | 940 |

### Step 1 — Precision

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{30}{30 + 10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75
$$

### Step 2 — Recall

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{30}{30 + 20} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.60
$$

### Step 3 — F1-score

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.60}{0.75 + 0.60} = \frac{0.90}{1.35} \approx 0.667
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU lab report should report **macro-averaged F1** across all relation classes, not just the positive class. Compute F1 for each class, then take the unweighted mean.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Feature Categories Used in Lab

| Feature Category | Specific Feature | Example Value | Why It Helps |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|--------------|
| Entity Type | Type of entity 1 | `PERSON` | Narrows possible relations |
| Entity Type | Type of entity 2 | `ORG` | Same as above |
| Surface | Tokens between entities | `is the CEO of` | Strong lexical cue |
| Surface | Entity 1 surface form | `Sundar Pichai` | Identity context |
| Positional | Token distance | `4` | Captures locality |
| Syntactic | Dependency path | `nsubj → appos → pobj` | Grammatic structure |
| Semantic | WordNet hypernym of middle word | `noun.person` | Generalizes lexical cues |
| Embedding | $\cos(\mathbf{e}_{e1}, \mathbf{e}_{e2})$ | `0.42` | Distributed similarity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture Flow

The diagram below shows the complete supervised learning pipeline that students must implement in the KTU NLP Lab for Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Sentence Splitter]
    B --> C[Named Entity Recognition NER]
    C --> D[Candidate Entity Pair Generator]
    D --> E[Feature Extractor Lexical Syntactic Positional]
    E --> F[Feature Vector x in R to the d]
    F --> G[Supervised Classifier SVM LogisticRegression]
    G --> H[Predicted Relation Label y hat]
    H --> I[Post processing Remove Duplicates]
    I --> J[Extracted Relations Knowledge Base]

    subgraph TRAINING PHASE
        K[Labeled Training Corpus] --> L[Same Pipeline A to F]
        L --> M[Model Parameter Learning minimize Cross Entropy]
        M --> G
    end

    subgraph EVALUATION PHASE
        H --> N[Compare y hat vs y true]
        N --> O[Compute Precision Recall F1]
    end
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Relation Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Input Sentence with Entities e1 and e2] --> Q{Entities in Same Sentence}
    Q -->|No| R[Return NO RELATION]
    Q -->|Yes| S[Extract Feature Vector]
    S --> T[Compute P y given x for each class k]
    T --> U{Max Probability above threshold tau}
    U -->|No| R
    U -->|Yes| V[Return argmax class as predicted relation]
```

## 4.3 Training Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Initialize weights W and bias b randomly] --> T2[Shuffle training instances]
    T2 --> T3[For each batch of instances]
    T3 --> T4[Forward pass compute logits and softmax]
    T4 --> T5[Compute cross entropy loss L]
    T5 --> T6[Backward pass compute gradients]
    T6 --> T7[Update W and b using gradient descent]
    T7 --> T8{Convergence or max epochs reached}
    T8 -->|No| T3
    T8 -->|Yes| T9[Save trained model for inference]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node ID Convention:** All Mermaid node IDs above use pure alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `T1`, `A`, `P`) and avoid reserved keywords. Labels use clean uppercase text with no inline markdown formatting to ensure successful Mermaid rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define Relation Detection and Relation Classification. How do they differ from Named Entity Recognition (NER)?**

**Model Answer:**
Relation Detection is the task of determining whether a semantic relationship exists between two given entities in a sentence, while Relation Classification is the task of assigning the correct relation type from a predefined set $\mathcal{R}$ to that pair. NER, in contrast, only identifies and types the entities themselves without considering pairwise semantic links. RDC operates *between* entities, whereas NER operates *on* entities in isolation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**List any three lexical features used in supervised relation extraction. Why is the feature "dependency path between entities" considered powerful?**

**Model Answer:**
Three lexical features: (1) entity type of $e_1$, (2) entity type of $e_2$, (3) tokens lying between the two entities. The dependency path is powerful because it captures the grammatical relationship between entities in a structured, language-independent form (e.g., `nsubj → appos → pobj`), allowing the classifier to generalize across surface-word variations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the end-to-end supervised learning pipeline for relation extraction. List and briefly describe the five major stages with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the gradient of the categorical cross-entropy loss with respect to the softmax scores and explain how the gradient update rule adjusts the model weights when a training instance is misclassified. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The five stages are:
1. **Sentence Splitting & Tokenization:** Input raw corpus; output list of tokenized sentences. Example: `"Apple Inc. is in California."` → `["Apple", "Inc.", "is", "in", "California", "."]`.
2. **Named Entity Recognition:** Identify and type entities. Example: `(Apple Inc., ORG)`, `(California, GPE)`.
3. **Candidate Pair Generation:** Produce all ordered/unordered pairs. With 2 entities → 1 candidate pair.
4. **Feature Extraction:** Convert each pair to a feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$. Example features: entity types, distance, tokens between.
5. **Classification & Post-processing:** Apply trained classifier; output relation label, e.g., `(Apple Inc., located_in, California)`.

#### Model Solution for (b):
The cross-entropy loss for a single instance with true class $c$ is:

$$
\ell_i = -\log \frac{\exp(z_c)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(z_j)} = -z_c + \log \sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(z_j)
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $z_k$:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial z_k} = \frac{\exp(z_k)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j)} - \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\} = p_k - \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\}
$$

Chain rule to weights:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial \mathbf{w}_k} = (p_k - \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\}) \cdot \mathbf{x}_i
$$

Update rule with learning rate $\eta$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_k \leftarrow \mathbf{w}_k - \eta \cdot (p_k - \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\}) \cdot \mathbf{x}_i
$$

**Interpretation for misclassification:** If the true class is $c$ but $p_c$ is small (say 0.1), then for $k = c$ the gradient is $0.1 - 1 = -0.9$, so the update is $\mathbf{w}_c \leftarrow \mathbf{w}_c + 0.9 \eta \mathbf{x}_i$, *increasing* the score for the correct class. Conversely, for a wrongly predicted class $k'$ with $p_{k'} = 0.7$, the gradient is $0.7 - 0 = 0.7$, so the update decreases its score. This is exactly the self-correcting mechanism of gradient descent.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how Precision, Recall, and F1-score are computed for a relation extraction system. A system predicts 50 relations, of which 40 are correct. The gold standard contains 60 true relations. Compute Precision, Recall, and F1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare feature-based and kernel-based supervised approaches to relation extraction. Which is more suitable for the KTU lab environment and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
- **Precision (P):** Of all relations the system predicted, how many are correct?

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80
$$

- **Recall (R):** Of all true relations in the gold set, how many did the system recover?

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{60} \approx 0.667
$$

- **F1-score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.667}{0.80 + 0.667} = \frac{1.067}{1.467} \approx 0.727
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):
| Aspect | Feature-Based | Kernel-Based |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Input representation | Explicit feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Structured objects (e.g., parse trees) |
| Classifier | SVM, MaxEnt, Neural Net | SVM with custom kernel (e.g., tree kernel) |
| Engineering effort | High — must handcraft features | Low — kernel computes similarity directly |
| Interpretability | High | Low |
| Lab suitability | **Highly suitable** | Computationally heavy; needs SVM-light + tree kernels |
